/form-publisher.title: OluwatobiMayowa
/form-publisher.description: An experienced data-driven product designer with about a decade of experience in interaction design, product strategy, and leadership.
p: Redesigning the installation flow for improved customer experience and higher business conversion. The new design saw +40% installations.
Research, UI design and validating product decisions with users/stakeholders.
Form Publisher, a Google Workspace add-on with 26 million downloads, transforms Google Form submissions into custom PDFs, Google Docs, or Microsoft Office documents.

Of the hundreds of installations that happen every day, only 32% lead to an activation. HMW simplify the installation-activation workflow so users can start generating their first file?In over 6 months of UX work, I observed current behavior, had several conversations with users, and shipped a redesign that saw close to 40% increase in installation rate.

The existing 4-step process was time-consuming and counter-intuitive.The user has to select the template and go through screens containing additional settings like destination folders, naming conventions, sharing list etc., none of which are mandatory for the file generation process to start.
Our team (a product designer, a technical writer, 2 software engineers, and a product manager) were on a mission to fix this, and I’d like to share how we did it.

I started by gaining context on the problem space by looking at the installation metrics, reading through customer frustrations collected by our support team, and reaching out to different teams. It became clear that the current flow:
Communicates a mandatory progress-like flow for settings that are indeed optional.
Too overwhelming for a first-time set-up
Of all daily installs, about 70% of users never generate a file.

Since I was new to Form Publisher, I had to familiarize myself with the add-on's existing workflow and other touchpoints. I mapped them all out and identified pain points relating to my gathered context.

The first concept was to show the mandatory screens, which were Template selection and Template/markers review. The other non-optional settings will be accessed at will by the user if they want extra configuration.
We know from our data however that only about 20% of the users will want more customization.

The first concept was linear and quick to complete, but it didn't feel as complete as it could have. User testing revealed that not all users want to create a form, and without a form, Form Publisher can't work. Historically, we have never made this clear in our design.

Instead of simply informing users that they need to create a form for FP to function, it's more effective to assist them in creating a sample form. To achieve this, I redesigned the installation process to incorporate a pre-selection screen where users can opt to create a demo form without having to engage with the form creation wizard.

To streamline the installation process, we removed the template selection step. Since users who don't want to create forms are unlikely to have custom templates, we provided sensible defaults and offer a preview option for users to review and customize if needed.
We shipped this as a high-confidence solution after rounds of UX reviews and feedback gathering.
The Get started allows for the user to select their template.

Once I understood the space better, I dug deeper into the framework. Historically, Form Publisher never had a dedicated designer and was only updated by developers. There was no existing source file, so I took a lot of screenshots and inspected a lot of code to develop source files, which provided a good insight into the structure.
There were a lot of general design principles that I leveraged as a baseline UX, to help create a stronger foundation for product success:
Rethink the layout to reduce clutter and emphasize priority actions
Unified elements sizes, positions, and visual treatments
Unified vertical spacing for clarity and better targets
Remove as many borders that tend to house individual elements, especially the horizontal lines between list elements
how is the sharing list currently being interpreted by our users?
how long can the list possibly get?
what are the highest possible character lengths we can encounter? e.t.c
Here, the second iteration was easier/faster for engineering to implement while still retaining the beneift of cleaner, more usable design than Iteration #1.
After launching the new installation flow, the installation rate surged from 32% to 71% in just four months.

While this improvement suggests we addressed issues related to linearity and clarity, I delved into the reasons why users still faced challenges during their initial installations. One key finding was that the add-on's speed, influenced by factors like internet connection, system specifications, and browser, contributed to user bounce rates.
In collaboration with leadership to address technical debt, I started ideating on some feedback screens to manage user expectations during loading times.
Feel free to reach out if you want to collaborate or simply have a chat.
LESS IS MORE IS MORE LIKE: MORE IS LESS, MORE OR LESS.

/unmc-sickle-cell-app.title: OluwatobiMayowa
/unmc-sickle-cell-app.description: An experienced data-driven product designer with about a decade of experience in interaction design, product strategy, and leadership.
p: Integrating the use of smartphones into an existing birth registration program for newborn screening and tracking of SCD
discovery, user interviews, interaction design, and engineering collaboration.

SCA (Sickle Cell Anaemia) is one of the most common and severe of all inherited hematological disorders, causing substantial morbidity and mortality worldwide. More than 3/4 of all children with SCA are born in sub-Saharan Africa – at least 270,000 births each year, with over half of these originating from Nigeria alone. The goal is to provide solutions for early SCA diagnosis through point-of-care testing and mobile device registries in health facilities.To integrate the use of smartphones into an existing birth registration program for newborn screening and tracking of babies with SCD (Sickle Cell Disease). The smartphone would be used for automation and transmission of read-out from a point-of-care device for the diagnosis of the SCD.

For this phase, I wanted to first learn more about the current way of collecting diagnosis information so I could have a better understanding of the issue, I proceeded with a visit to the clinic for the first week to observe current ways of doing things. Then I came up with a general high-level overview of the process.
Together with te Product Manager, we broke down the obvious tasks into user stories. I then started creating skeleton user flows on paper that satisfies those stories.
I created several wireframes in Balsamiq to test concepts, flows, and information architecture. These were validated by printing them on blank sheets and walking 2-4 doctors through on each visit.

While testing, I had to change some texts to reflect the medical domain. Some notions also changed. Notably, because babies would have issues with fingerprint, the fingerprint search only works for the guardian/parents. And since they could also have more than one child, my initial 1-1 mapping had to change to many-many so I could compensate for this in the design.

Designed for unanticipated occurrences: Empty states, Max states, Error states e.t.c I leveraged material design language to allow for ease of component conversion by developers and created compelling/elegant interfaces that are easy to use.

The initial assumption for 1-1 mapping for the search wasn’t scalable, we ended up with a realistic many-many scenario and then ideated physical cards as backup.We had several discussions around the choice for split view or full view for the patient list landing page, we ended up with a full view.Communicating high fidelity design interactions to developers was difficult at first: Material design possible interactions and Principle came to the rescue, This also made it possible to test high fi prototypes with users.
Feel free to reach out if you want to collaborate or simply have a chat.

Currently leading personalization efforts within the Activation team as a Principal Product Designer.
Zalando is a leading European online platform for fashion and lifestyle e-commerce. It has 51million customers in 25 countries.

The feed surfaced 70–100 campaigns daily, overwhelming users with noise. Personalization relied on campaign ranking alone, resulting in endless scroll, repetitive experiences, and poor visibility of new campaigns. HMW rethink the feed to address these?
Reducing friction, boosting security, and increasing registration by 17.8%
HMW simplify authentication experience across 2 propositions while respecting their unique user needs?
Turning fragmented acquisition channels into tailored landing experiences

65% of traffic came from paid channels with varying intents, rules and brand restrictions, yet <1% convert. HMW leverage these intents to create first-session experiences that feel relevant to users while respecting brand rules?
Built and maintained a multi-themed multi-platform design system with a Figma-Token Studio-GitHub workflow.
Raised the bar for design and shaped product direction.
Busha is the leading regulated cryptocurrency exchange for Africans. It has already onboarded over 1 million users into the crypto economy.

/carbon-app.title: OluwatobiMayowa
/carbon-app.description: An experienced data-driven product designer with about a decade of experience in interaction design, product strategy, and leadership.
p: Led the mobile interface redesign and experience revamp of a top African digital bank with over 1 million users.

I handled the UX and visual design, but we collaborated closely on the user research. We jointly developed research questions, and I conducted the sessions while my co-designer observed and took notes.

Paylater was originally an online lending platform that provides short-term loans to help cover unexpected expenses. As the app grew beyond offering loans, a new name and brand refresh became imperative, these birthed Carbon!

After the identity rebrand was concluded by external consultants, there was an urgent need to revamp the interface and experience of the app while capturing newly planned features like free credit reports, investments, single transaction history, and wallet.
Gathering a single source of truth for the current screens

The lack of an intentional UX effort in the Paylater app meant that there was no Sketch file, PSD or any tangible resource housing those screens. With the aid of some user flows from engineering (Android Studio to the rescue), I took 100’s of screenshots on the live app to form the only concrete resource we could use as discussion points.

Prior to the redesign, there were no established UI standards that covered the product, or anything that served as a ‘single source of truth’ for the team. In fact, I was referencing the real app, taking screenshots and re-drawing what was already live.Understanding the growth trajectory of the business, and it’s expanding product footprint, I saw an opportunity to define how UI can be better managed and in a more collaborative way. This necessitated a creation of a component based design system.
Testing key screens to better decide if redesign is worth the time and effort

Whenever I noticed where UX can be improved, I will create quick sketches and call for a team discussion. In the case below, it was something customer suppport says users are confused about: Hundreds of users calling about their inability to withdraw from their accounts.
I found this to be a UX issue (discoverability) not a functionality problem.

During the rebrand of Paylater to Carbon, I had to update about 30 micro-interactions to fit the new style guide. All of these animations were created in GIF format and there were no source files available to update the colours.After trying to look for several workarounds, I dedicated a week to rethinking the whole animations, recreated them in After Effects, redesigned some entirely and converted them to JSON via BodyMovin/Lottie.The result was a migration from static moving images to mathematically calculated vectors that rendered natively on the Android engine. The app felt lighter overall, we saved about 7 megabytes in app size, animations felt more crisp on user devices and we now have reusable files that can be easily tweaked for years to come.
Feel free to reach out if you want to collaborate or simply have a chat.
LESS IS MORE IS MORE LIKE: MORE IS LESS, MORE OR LESS.

/busha-yield.title: OluwatobiMayowa
/busha-yield.description: An experienced data-driven product designer with about a decade of experience in interaction design, product strategy, and leadership.
p: Crafting the end-to-end UX for users to get interests on their crypto savings
ui design, ux research, field research
Busha is an African digital assets company with over 500k users. The basic offering of the product was for buying/selling cryptocurrencies.
HMW allow users earn interest on their cryptocurrencies to edge against devaluation.
Open up a new revenue channel while increasing user retention.
Product features are expressed over a 3 nav system of- Explore (news/action banners)- Portfolio (crypto list/values)- Account (profile/settings)
To kickstart, I experimented with a ton of savings and investment features across crypto and FIAT domains. This gathering of ideas from the wild set a pace for inspiration.
The product direction was shaped by existing FIAT savings user behavior, regulatory framework and adaptation of a new feature to an existing app.
The final user flow I made looked something like this. The rationale was to attempt a 4-step process that is both natural and intuitive.
We had a pretty mature design system, so the initial questions were “Where would it make sense for people to initiate the Yield flow from? , “What should the landing page they see look like?” e.t.c
We went with the 3rd option because:
A dedicated tab boxes our real estate for future launches of Spend, Card and Loans features.
Semantically and technically, yield and spot balances make up your portfolio, and Option 1 doesn’t make such balance referencing possible,
Based on user research, spot is for immediate utility and Yield is more long-term, separating them reduces cognitive load.
Use the current tab and show Yield plans as part of Wallet list.
Turn Wallet bottom tab into Portfolio and make Spot, Yield top tabs
The Yield section is empty by default.
Plan creation is initiated by clicking the Subscribe button
Review the plan, agree to terms
When the amount entered is greater than the available balance, a top up prompt is presented.
Several explorations were made to enable easy feature discovery by the user.
Design explorations range from explanatory story sliders to a static explainer screen and a recommendation banner on the homepage.
After considering user goals, utility and function, a combination of option 1 and option 3 were shipped.
Other manifestations of the finalized solution.
Feel free to reach out if you want to collaborate or simply have a chat.

/.title: OluwatobiMayowa
/.description: An experienced data-driven product designer with about a decade of experience in interaction design, product strategy, and leadership.
h1: The best way to PREDICT the future is to DESIGN it.
PREDICT the future is to DESIGN it.
h5: From designing a sickle cell management app in Northern Nigeria 8 years ago, to serving as founding designer at a crypto startup that raised $4.2m and grew to 1M customers with a 4.6★ app rating, to now shaping growth and personalization at Zalando for 50M+ customers across Europe. My craft, systems thinking, and influence have evolved, yet curiosity and evidence-based design remain at the core of my practice.
h6: I’ve been working with Tobi for over 5 years. He’s one of the most gifted designers I’ve had the pleasure of working with. As the founding designer for Busha, he’s helped set a design bar, mentored incoming designers and created a system that has helped us scale both our product team and customer expectations.

Tobi was a fractional hire to help us level up the aesthetics of our remittance mobile apps after we got our Series A. His impact went beyond the app redesign. Due to his technical expertise, he was able to increase the product design literacy of the entire team. Tactically, he created our cross-platform design system and ensured adoption.

He excels at communicating effectively with cross-functional stakeholders, including product managers, engineers, marketing teams, and executives. He does this by triangulating multiple research data while clearly articulating design decisions, addressing concerns, and building consensus among different departments.

Tobi initially designed our Google Workspace add-on, Form Publisher. Later, we expanded into AI tools and brought him on board. Despite the new domain, he adeptly handled intricate user needs, conflicting stakeholder demands, and changing market conditions, making informed decisions under pressure.

We were colleagues at AZA. His approach to peer designing was stellar. He was the go-to-person for prototyping issues, and the the humility with which he transfers his knowledge is exemplary. He fluently executes a solid range of design skills while managing different group dynamics. He also interviewed & motivated me to join the team.
p: I’M watching youtube, LIFTING WEIGHTS OR SKIING
Feel free to reach out if you want to collaborate or simply have a chat.
LESS IS MORE IS MORE LIKE: MORE IS LESS, MORE OR LESS.
